text: I do not know whether you have been inconvenienced by a friend stopping sending you his monthly religious texts, but I have. Mine has done so for a year, and I am at a loss to guess the reason. Perhaps he thought I had had enough. Perhaps he had grown tired of the practice. Perhaps he did not care to spend a penny a month on me. Perhaps he had some motive which I cannot fathom. At all events, here am I, left with a long series of texts on hand which I would fain dispose of. Shall I trouble you to find me a purchaser?

text: Sleeping and waking are states so opposite, that it is difficult to conceive how they can both belong to the same subject, at the same time. Yet experience shows us, that a man may be, at once, awake and asleep. The transition from wakefulness to slumber is gradual; and, in general, perfect rest of mind and body is not obtained, until sense and thought are suspended. When slumber has thoroughly possessed the faculties, the man is completely passive; he is then wholly subject to the influence of his dreams, be they agreeable or painful. prompt_text: my coworker is selling a $400 productivity course that he says will "transform my output"
text: My coworker is selling me a course which he says will "transform" my output, and I am inclined to think well of it. But, if it transforms my output, it must first of all transform me; and the question is, Am I in need of transformation? Am I working at present in a manner profitable to myself and to society? and, if so, can the proposed course better me? On the other hand, if I am working unprofitably, is the course likely to set me on the road to profit? Before I commit myself to the purchase, I must be clear upon these points.
